summary: Deliberately vulnerable demo app with broken access control, path traversal, hardcoded secret, debug mode, and command injection patterns.

findings.title: User-controlled org_id allows cross-tenant invoice access
findings.summary: The invoice endpoint trusts org_id from the query string instead of deriving authorization from the authenticated user.
findings.impact: An authenticated user may retrieve invoices belonging to another organization if they know or guess invoice identifiers and organization IDs.

preconditions: Attacker can access the invoice endpoint
Attacker can guess or enumerate invoice IDs
exploitability.safe_reproduction: Run only against the local deliberately vulnerable demo app with seeded demo data.

evidence.reasoning: Organization scope is taken from user-controlled query parameters and compared to invoice data rather than to the authenticated session.

findings.recommended_fix: Derive org_id from current_user() or server-side session claims and enforce the invoice lookup with that trusted organization ID.
suggested_tests: User from org_a cannot access inv_2
Supplying org_id in the query string is ignored

findings.title: Download endpoint permits path traversal outside reports directory
findings.summary: The download route joins a user-controlled filename to the reports directory without normalization or allowlist checks.
findings.impact: A local demo user can request paths such as ../app.py and read files outside the intended reports directory if the process can access them.

preconditions: Attacker can reach the local demo download endpoint
Target file is readable by the Flask process
attack_path: The app returns a file outside the reports directory
exploitability.safe_reproduction: Run only against the local deliberately vulnerable demo app with toy files.

evidence.reasoning: User input is appended to a base directory without resolving and enforcing that the final path remains inside the allowed directory.

findings.recommended_fix: Resolve the requested path, enforce that it stays under an absolute reports directory, and preferably serve only allowlisted report IDs.

findings.title: Hardcoded Flask secret key in source
findings.summary: The Flask SECRET_KEY is committed as a static development value.
findings.impact: If copied into a real deployment, attackers who know the key may forge or tamper with signed Flask session data.

preconditions: Application uses Flask signed sessions or related signing
exploitability.safe_reproduction: Treat this as static evidence only; do not attempt to forge sessions on systems you do not own.

evidence.reasoning: Secret material is embedded directly in source code rather than loaded from a deployment secret store.

findings.recommended_fix: Load SECRET_KEY from environment or a secret manager and fail closed if it is absent in non-development environments.
suggested_tests: Application refuses to start in production without SECRET_KEY
Development defaults cannot be used when FLASK_ENV=production

findings.title: Debug mode enabled in application entry point
findings.summary: The app starts Flask with debug=True, which is unsafe outside a local demo environment.
findings.impact: If copied into deployment, debug mode may expose interactive debugger behavior, stack traces, environment details, and unsafe development settings.

preconditions: The development server is exposed beyond localhost or copied into deployment
An exception or debugger route is reachable
attack_path: Inspect debug output or debugger exposure
Use leaked details to deepen compromise
exploitability.safe_reproduction: Do not expose Flask debug mode publicly; verify statically or on a private local demo only.

evidence.reasoning: Debug mode is explicitly enabled in the application entry point.

findings.recommended_fix: Use environment-specific configuration and ensure debug is disabled by default and in all production-like contexts.
suggested_tests: Entrypoint reads debug setting from explicit local-only env flag

findings.title: Unsafe shell command construction in image resize endpoint
findings.summary: The resize endpoint interpolates a JSON filename into an os.system command string.
findings.impact: If reachable in a real service, attacker-controlled filename content could alter shell command execution under the application user.

preconditions: The app runs the endpoint on a host with shell access to convert
attack_path: Shell parses injected syntax in os.system
exploitability.safe_reproduction: Use only static review or a disposable local demo; do not provide deployable payloads or run against third-party systems.

evidence.reasoning: User-controlled filename flows into shell=True-like command execution through os.system without argument separation or validation.

findings.recommended_fix: Avoid shell invocation. Use a library API or subprocess.run with a list of fixed arguments, validate filenames, and confine file access to an uploads directory.
suggested_tests: Filenames containing shell metacharacters are rejected
Resize uses argument list or library call
Output path is fixed and not user-controlled
